title: Common cold - NHS
text:
es You can buy cough and cold medicines from pharmacies or supermarkets.
A pharmacist can advise you on the best medicine, such as:
medicines like paracetamol or ibuprofen to ease aches or lower a temperature decongestant nasal sprays, drops or tablets to unblock your nose (decongestants should not be used by children under 6) vapour rubs to help ease chesty coughs in babies and small children You can buy nasal sprays without a prescription, but they should not be used for more than a week as this can make your symptoms worse.
Do not use other cough and cold medicines if you're also taking paracetamol and ibuprofen tablets as you may take more medicine than you should.
Some cough and cold medicines are also not suitable for babies, children and pregnant women.
There's little evidence that supplements such as vitamin C, echinacea or garlic prevent colds or help you get better more quickly.
Find a pharmacy 
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
you have a high temperature for more than 3 days your cold symptoms get worse your temperature is very high, or you feel hot, cold or shivery you feel short of breath or have chest pain you're worried about your child's cold symptoms your cold symptoms do not get better after 10 days you have a cough for more than 3 weeks you get cold symptoms and you have a long-term medical condition (for example, diabetes, or a heart, lung or kidney condition
